title: Cognitive Reframing
description: Identify and challenge irrational or maladaptive thoughts.
instructions: Identify a negative thought you're having.
Ask yourself: 'Is there evidence for this thought?'
Ask: 'Is there evidence against it?'
Try to come up with a more balanced, realistic perspective.

title: The Worry Time Technique
description: Dedicate a specific time to worry so it doesn't consume your whole day.
instructions: Pick a 15-minute slot during the day as your 'Worry Time'.
When worries pop up outside this time, write them down and tell yourself 'I'll worry about this later.'
During Worry Time, go through your list and address the concerns.
When the 15 minutes are up, stop and move on to your next activity.

title: Exposure Hierarchy
description: Gradually face fears starting from the least scary.
instructions: List situations that trigger your anxiety.
Rate each on a scale of 1-10 (1 = least scary, 10 = most scary).
Start by facing the situation rated a 1 or 2.
Practice staying in that situation until your anxiety drops by half.
Move up the hierarchy as you feel more confident.

title: Behavioral Activation
description: Increase engagement in positive activities to improve mood.
instructions: List 5 small activities that used to bring you joy or a sense of accomplishment.
Schedule one of these activities for tomorrow.
Commit to doing it regardless of your mood.
Afterward, rate how you felt before and after the activity.

title: Gratitude Journaling
description: Shift focus toward positive experiences.
instructions: Every evening, write down three things you are grateful for from that day.
Be specific (e.g., 'A warm cup of tea', not just 'tea').
Reflect on why those things happened.
Notice how your mood shifts as you focus on the positive.

title: The 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding Technique
description: Bring your focus back to the present moment.
instructions: Identify 5 things you can see.
Identify 4 things you can touch.
Identify 3 things you can hear.
Identify 2 things you can smell.
Identify 1 thing you can taste.

title: Setting Boundaries
description: Define limits to protect your energy and time.
instructions: Identify one area where you feel overextended (e.g., work, social life).
Determine a specific boundary you need (e.g., 'No work emails after 7 PM').
Communicate this boundary clearly and politely to those it affects.
Practice saying 'no' to requests that violate this boundary.

title: Affirmation Practice
description: Counteract negative self-talk with positive statements.
instructions: Write down a common negative thought you have about yourself.
Turn it into a positive, realistic affirmation (e.g., 'I am learning and growing').
Repeat this affirmation to yourself in the mirror every morning.
Try to find evidence in your life that supports the affirmation.

title: Meaning Reconstruction
description: Find ways to integrate loss into your life story.
instructions: Write about a positive quality or lesson you learned from the person or thing you lost.
Consider how you can honor that quality in your current life.
Reflect on how your perspective on life has changed through this experience.
Look for small ways to find purpose in your day-to-day life.

title: Active Listening
description: Improve communication and empathy in relationships.
instructions: During your next conversation, focus entirely on what the other person is saying.
Wait for them to finish before formulating your response.
Paraphrase what they said to ensure you understood ('So, what I'm hearing is...').
Ask clarifying questions instead of making assumptions.

title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
description: Systematically tense and relax muscle groups.
instructions: Find a quiet place to sit or lie down.
Start with your toes: tense them for 5 seconds, then release for 10.
Move up to your calves, thighs, stomach, and so on, up to your face.
Notice the difference between the tension and relaxation.

title: Probability Estimation
description: Realistically assess the likelihood of feared outcomes.
instructions: Identify a 'worst-case scenario' you're worried about.
Rate the likelihood of it happening from 0-100%.
Think of 3 alternative, more likely outcomes.
Rate the likelihood of those outcomes.
Compare the ratings and notice if your fear is proportionate.

title: The Decatastrophizing 'What If'
description: Follow the 'what if' chain to its logical conclusion.
instructions: Ask yourself: 'What if [feared event] happens?'
Then ask: 'And what if THAT happens?'
Continue until you reach the end of the chain.
Then ask: 'How would I cope? Who could help me?'
Realize that even the worst-case scenario is survivable.

title: Thought Stopping
description: Interruption of repetitive negative thoughts.
instructions: When a repetitive anxious thought occurs, visualize a big red STOP sign.
Say 'Stop!' out loud or in your head.
Immediately shift your attention to a neutral or positive thought.
Repeat as necessary until the loop is broken.

title: Labeling Feelings
description: Reduce the intensity of emotions by naming them.
instructions: When you feel a strong emotion, pause.
Say to yourself: 'I am feeling [anxiety/fear/tension].'
This simple act of labeling activates the prefrontal cortex and calms the amygdala.
Observe the feeling without trying to change it.

title: Pleasant Activity Scheduling
description: Plan specific times for enjoyment.
instructions: Look at your calendar for the coming week.
Block out 30 minutes for a 'pleasure appointment' (e.g., reading, walking, hobby).
Treat this appointment as just as important as a work meeting.
Reflect on how it felt to prioritize your well-being.

title: Identifying Strengths
description: Counteract self-criticism by focusing on capabilities.
instructions: List 5 things you've accomplished in your life, big or small.
Identify the strengths you used to achieve them (e.g., persistence, kindness).
Write down how you can use one of those strengths today.
Remind yourself of these strengths when you're feeling low.

title: Self-Compassion Break
description: Treat yourself with the same kindness as a friend.
instructions: Acknowledge a moment of suffering ('This is a moment of suffering').
Remind yourself that struggle is part of the human experience ('Suffering is a part of life').
Offer yourself kindness ('May I be kind to myself').
Place your hand over your heart for comfort.

title: The ABC Model
description: Understand the link between events, beliefs, and consequences.
instructions: A (Activating Event): What happened?
B (Beliefs): What did you tell yourself about the event?
C (Consequences): How did you feel and act as a result?
Identify if 'B' was irrational and try to replace it with a healthier belief.

title: Values-Based Living
description: Align actions with core values.
instructions: Identify 3 core values (e.g., connection, creativity, health).
For each value, list one small action you can take this week.
Notice the sense of fulfillment that comes from acting in alignment with your values.
Adjust your schedule to include more values-based activities.

title: Mindful Breathing
description: Using the breath as an anchor to the present.
instructions: Sit comfortably and close your eyes.
Focus on the sensation of breath entering and leaving your body.
When your mind wanders, gently bring it back to the breath.
Continue for 5 minutes.

title: Single-Tasking
description: Reduce cognitive load by doing one thing at a time.
instructions: Pick one task (e.g., eating lunch, washing dishes).
Put away distractions like your phone or TV.
Focus entirely on the sensory experience of the task.
Notice if you feel calmer and more focused.

title: The 'Not-To-Do' List
description: Identify and eliminate unnecessary stressors.
instructions: List tasks or habits that drain your energy but aren't essential.
Choose one to 'stop doing' for the next week.
Observe the impact on your stress levels.
Protect your time for activities that recharge you.

title: Body Scan
description: Release physical tension from head to toe.
instructions: Lie down comfortably.
Slowly scan your body for areas of tightness or discomfort.
As you breathe in, imagine oxygen flowing to that area.
As you breathe out, imagine the tension melting away.

title: Nature Connection
description: Lower cortisol levels through immersion in nature.
instructions: Spend 15 minutes in a green space (park, garden, even near a houseplant).
Notice the colors, textures, and sounds around you.
Engage your senses fully in the natural environment.
Breathe deeply and enjoy the stillness.

title: The Energy Audit
description: Identify what drains and what fuels you.
instructions: Track your activities for 24 hours.
Label each activity as either 'D' (Draining) or 'F' (Fueling).
Look for ways to reduce 'D' and increase 'F' in your next 24 hours.
Notice any patterns in your energy levels throughout the day.

title: Digital Detox Hour
description: Give your brain a break from constant information.
instructions: Choose one hour before bed to put away all screens.
Engage in an offline activity like reading, journaling, or stretching.
Notice how this affects your sleep quality and morning mood.
Slowly increase the duration of your detox.

title: Micro-Breaks
description: Prevent fatigue through short, frequent rests.
instructions: Set a timer for every 50 minutes of work.
Take a 5-minute break when the timer goes off.
Step away from your desk, stretch, or look out a window.
Avoid checking your phone during these breaks.

title: Delegation Practice
description: Reduce burden by sharing responsibilities.
instructions: Identify one task you're doing that someone else could handle.
Ask for help or delegate the task clearly.
Let go of the need to have it done 'your way'.
Use the saved time for rest or a refueling activity.

title: Re-evaluating 'Musts'
description: Challenge the pressure of internal demands.
instructions: Write down 3 things you feel you 'must' do today.
Change 'must' to 'would like to' (e.g., 'I would like to finish this report').
Notice if the pressure decreases when you use more flexible language.
Decide if all three are truly necessary or can be postponed.

title: The 'I' Statement
description: Express needs without being accusatory.
instructions: Instead of 'You always...', say 'I feel... when... because...'
Example: 'I feel overwhelmed when the dishes are left because I have to do them alone.'
This reduces defensiveness in the listener.
Practice this next time you need to address a conflict.

title: Perspective-Taking
description: Build empathy by seeing through another's eyes.
instructions: Think of a person you're having conflict with.
Try to imagine three reasons they might be acting that way.
Consider their stressors, background, and goals.
Notice if your frustration softens when you consider their perspective.

title: Positive Tracking
description: Focus on strengths in your relationships.
instructions: For one week, specifically look for things your partner/friend does well.
Write down at least one positive interaction per day.
Express your appreciation for these things out loud.
Notice how this changes the tone of your relationship.

title: Conflict De-escalation
description: Techniques to keep disagreements productive.
instructions: When a conversation gets heated, agree to a 20-minute 'Time Out'.
Separate and do something calming (not thinking about the argument).
Come back together and resume with a lower volume and slower pace.
Focus on finding a solution rather than being 'right'.

title: Shared Values Activity
description: Strengthen bond through common ground.
instructions: With a partner or friend, each list your top 5 values.
Compare lists and find where they overlap.
Discuss one activity you can do together that honors a shared value.
Commit to doing that activity this month.

title: The Inner Critic vs. The Inner Coach
description: Transform internal dialogue.
instructions: Identify a common self-critical thought ('I'm so lazy').
Imagine what a supportive coach would say instead ('You've been under a lot of stress, it's okay to rest').
Write down the coach's version and read it whenever the critic appears.
Practice speaking to yourself with the same encouragement you'd give a friend.

title: Skill-Building Action
description: Increase confidence through mastery.
instructions: Choose one small skill you'd like to improve (e.g., cooking a new dish, learning a word).
Dedicate 15 minutes to practicing it today.
Acknowledge your progress, no matter how small.
Notice how achieving a small goal improves your self-perception.

title: Mirror Work
description: Improve self-acceptance through direct eye contact.
instructions: Look at yourself in the mirror for 1 minute.
Instead of looking for 'flaws', look into your own eyes.
Say something kind to your reflection ('I am worthy of love and respect').
Notice any discomfort and breathe through it.

title: The 'Success' Folder
description: Create a physical or digital record of achievements.
instructions: Collect screenshots of compliments, photos of accomplishments, or notes of thanks.
When you're feeling inadequate, spend 10 minutes looking through this folder.
Remind yourself that these are evidence of your value and impact.
Add to the folder whenever something positive happens.

title: Limiting Comparison
description: Protect your self-esteem from social media traps.
instructions: Identify one social media account that makes you feel 'less than'.
Unfollow or mute that account for one week.
Notice if your mood and self-worth improve.
Replace scrolling time with a self-care activity.

title: Continuing Bonds
description: Maintain a healthy connection with what was lost.
instructions: Identify a way to keep a positive connection to the deceased/lost item.
This could be doing an activity they loved, visiting a favorite spot, or wearing something of theirs.
Allow yourself to feel both the sadness and the connection.
Understand that grief doesn't have to mean 'letting go' completely.

title: Letter Writing
description: Express unspoken thoughts and feelings.
instructions: Write a letter to the person or situation you are grieving.
Say everything you didn't get to say—your anger, your love, your regrets.
You don't have to show this to anyone.
You can choose to keep it, bury it, or burn it as a ritual of release.

title: Rituals of Remembrance
description: Create structure for honoring loss.
instructions: Choose a specific date or time to honor your loss.
Light a candle, look at photos, or share stories with a friend.
Having a set time for grief can help it feel less overwhelming at other times.
Notice how the ritual helps you process the emotion.

title: Grief Waves Visualization
description: Understand the ebb and flow of intense emotion.
instructions: Visualize your grief as waves in the ocean.
Some are huge and crashing, others are small ripples.
When a big wave comes, imagine yourself floating on top of it rather than fighting it.
Know that the wave will eventually pass and the water will calm again.

title: Self-Care for Mourners
description: Prioritize physical needs during emotional distress.
instructions: Grief is physically exhausting. Check your basics: hydration, sleep, food.
Commit to one 'basic' self-care act today (e.g., drinking 8 glasses of water).
Lower your expectations of yourself for a while.
Ask for help with chores or responsibilities if needed.

title: The 3-3-3 Rule
description: Quick grounding during high anxiety.
instructions: Name 3 things you see.
Name 3 sounds you hear.
Move 3 parts of your body (e.g., fingers, toes, shoulders).
This refocuses the brain on the physical world.

title: Progressive Count Breath
description: Calm the nervous system with rhythmic breathing.
instructions: Breathe in for a count of 4.
Breathe out for a count of 4.
Repeat, increasing to a count of 6 on the next round.
Continue up to a count of 8, then work your way back down to 4.

title: Personal Bill of Rights
description: Assert your right to well-being.
instructions: Read through these rights: 'I have the right to say no', 'I have the right to be treated with respect', 'I have the right to make mistakes'.
Write down two more rights that feel important to you.
Keep this list somewhere you can see it.
Remind yourself of these rights when you feel pressured or undervalued.

title: The 'Opposite Action'
description: Change emotions by acting contrary to them.
instructions: Identify the urge associated with your mood (e.g., depression makes you want to stay in bed).
Do the exact opposite (e.g., get up and take a shower).
The physical action sends a signal to the brain that the 'mood' is not in control.
Notice if the mood shifts slightly after the action.

title: Defining Success
description: Create personal metrics for a 'good day'.
instructions: Redefine a successful day without including work metrics.
Example: 'Success today is drinking enough water and reading for 10 minutes.'
Focus on achieving these personal metrics.
Release the guilt of not meeting external, draining standards.
